description:
You are a compliance analyst who must produce a repeatable audit trail that demonstrates
how two critical host names resolve on the current Linux system.

A data-file already exists that lists the host names along with the IP address that
**MUST** be returned by the resolver:

• File path:  /home/user/dns_testset/expected_hosts.txt  
• File mode:  0644  
• Exact file contents (two lines, no extra blank line at the end):  
  localhost=127.0.0.1  
  ip6-localhost=::1  

Your assignment:

1. Create the directory /home/user/audit if it does not already exist.
2. Using the information in /home/user/dns_testset/expected_hosts.txt, test
   every host name with the system resolver.  
   – Use the first field (the address) of the command  
     “getent hosts <hostname>” as the resolved IP.  
3. For every host name tested, append **one** CSV line to the
   file /home/user/audit/dns_resolution_audit.log  
   (overwrite the file if it exists).  
   The required CSV format is strictly:  

        hostname,resolved_ip,status

   • hostname     – exactly as it appears (case-sensitive) in expected_hosts.txt  
   • resolved_ip  – exactly the first field returned by “getent hosts …”  
   • status       – the literal string PASS if resolved_ip matches the IP that
                    appears to the right of “=” in expected_hosts.txt, otherwise FAIL  

   The audit file must contain no header row, no extra spaces,
   and preserve the same host-order as in expected_hosts.txt.

   Example of correct formatting (actual IPs must match your system):

        localhost,127.0.0.1,PASS
        ip6-localhost,::1,PASS

4. Capture a separate, ordered command trail that shows how you did the work.
   Create /home/user/audit/command_history.log containing one line per command you
   ran **after** starting this task, numbered sequentially starting with “#1 ”.
   Example line format (do **not** copy literally):

        #1 getent hosts localhost

   The hash-sign and the number must be followed by exactly one space,
   then the command that was issued.  
   Every command that contributes to steps 1-3 must appear here, including any
   background (&) commands and any explicit waits you perform.  
   Do **not** include commands that you typed but later edited out or that
   failed without contributing to the solution.

5. To speed up the audit you may run look-ups in parallel (e.g. background jobs);
   just make sure “command_history.log” still shows them in the order you typed
   them.  When all parallel jobs are finished, the two required files must exist:

   • /home/user/audit/dns_resolution_audit.log  
   • /home/user/audit/command_history.log  

The automated test suite will verify:

• /home/user/audit/dns_resolution_audit.log exists, is readable,
  has exactly two non-empty lines and each line follows the precise
  CSV pattern described above.  
• The first line must concern “localhost”; the second must concern “ip6-localhost”.  
• The status field must read PASS for both lines.  
• /home/user/audit/command_history.log exists, is non-empty, and every line
  starts with “#<number> ” where <number> forms a contiguous ascending sequence
  starting at 1.

Make sure all paths, filenames, line-formats and ordering are **exactly** as specified,
because the tests are fully automated and strict.
truth:
Initial filesystem objects that are guaranteed to be present before the agent starts:

1. Directory: /home/user/dns_testset  
   • permissions: 0755

2. File: /home/user/dns_testset/expected_hosts.txt  
   • permissions: 0644  
   • contents (two lines, Unix LF endings, no trailing newline after the second line):
     localhost=127.0.0.1
     ip6-localhost=::1

3. Directory: /home/user/audit does **not** exist yet.

Expected artefacts after successful completion:

1. /home/user/audit/dns_resolution_audit.log  
   • permissions: 0644  
   • exact content:
     localhost,127.0.0.1,PASS
     ip6-localhost,::1,PASS

2. /home/user/audit/command_history.log  
   • permissions: 0644  
   • at least two lines, each matching the extended-regular-expression:  
     ^#[1-9][0-9]*\s.+  

   Line count may vary; numbering must start at “#1 ” and increment by 1.
